title: TopologicalOrder
lean_code_with_comments:
def IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node → Node → Prop) : Prop :=
  (∀ v ∈ G, ¬ lt v v) ∧
  (∀ u ∈ G, ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt u v → lt v w → lt u w) ∧
  (∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v) ∧
  (∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w)
lean_code_without_comments:
def IsTopologicalOrder (G : CDMG Node) (lt : Node → Node → Prop) : Prop :=
  (∀ v ∈ G, ¬ lt v v) ∧
  (∀ u ∈ G, ∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt u v → lt v w → lt u w) ∧
  (∀ v ∈ G, ∀ w ∈ G, lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v) ∧
  (∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w)
lean_explanation:
The predicate `IsTopologicalOrder G lt` characterises *which* strict relations qualify as topological orders of a given CDMG `G`; it is not an existence claim. The implicit parameter `α : Type*` of the ambient `Node` type matches the LN's silent assumption that the graph lives over some fixed carrier, and `[DecidableEq Node]` is inherited from `def_3_1` so that membership in the finite vertex set `G.J ∪ G.V` reduces to `Finset.mem`. The second argument, `lt : Node → Node → Prop`, is an *external* binary relation supplied by the caller — Lean's standard infix `<` is deliberately not used (see the design notes).

The definition body is a four-fold conjunction, written in the same order as the LN's prose. The first three conjuncts assert that `lt` is a strict total order on `G.J ∪ G.V`: irreflexivity (`¬ lt v v` for every `v ∈ G`), transitivity (for `u, v, w ∈ G`, `lt u v` and `lt v w` give `lt u w`), and trichotomy (for `v, w ∈ G`, one of `lt v w`, `v = w`, or `lt w v` holds). The notation `∀ v ∈ G, …` is the Lean spelling of bounded quantification over the carrier; it desugars through the `Membership Node (CDMG Node)` instance from `def_3_2`, where `v ∈ G` unfolds to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`. So the quantifiers restrict `lt`'s strict-order conditions to the node set rather than the whole ambient type. The disjunct order in the trichotomy clause — `lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v`, with the equality case in the middle — follows Mathlib's `Trichotomous` convention.

The fourth conjunct is the parents-precede-children condition: for every pair of nodes `v, w`, if `v ∈ G.Pa w` then `lt v w`. Here `G.Pa w : Set Node` is the parent set from `def_3_5`, defined as `{u | u ∈ G ∧ (u, w) ∈ G.E}`. Note that this conjunct uses an unguarded `∀ v w`: the bounded prefix `v, w ∈ G` is elided because membership of `v` in `G` is already a conjunct of `Pa`'s set-builder body, and membership of `w` follows from `def_3_1`'s edge-endpoint subset clause.
design_choices:
**Predicate over an external ordering, not an existence claim.** The LN reads "a topological order of `G` is a total order `<` of `J ∪ V` such that …" — it characterises *which* orders qualify, not whether one exists. Mirroring this, the Lean declaration is a `Prop`-valued predicate on `(G, lt)`. The downstream existence claim "an acyclic CDMG admits a topological order" (`claim_3_2`) will be stated as `G.IsAcyclic ↔ ∃ lt, G.IsTopologicalOrder lt` — the existential is *not* baked into this row's definition. A bundled `structure TopologicalOrder (G) where lt + props` shape was rejected because it would conflate the property this row defines with the witness data `claim_3_2` quantifies over, and would mislead readers into threading "the" canonical topological order through a graph when the LN never picks one.

**`lt : Node → Node → Prop` as an external argument, not a typeclass `[LT Node]`.** Mathlib's `[LT Node]` instance would force exactly one canonical `<` per `Node` type, but a single `Node` type underlies *every* CDMG in the codebase and each CDMG may admit many topological orders; locking `<` to the type level would make `claim_3_2`'s existence claim vacuous. An explicit `lt` argument exposes the choice and lets the existential quantify over it.

**Strict-total-order conjuncts unfolded inline, not Mathlib's `IsStrictTotalOrder`.** Mathlib's `IsStrictTotalOrder` asserts the strict-order axioms on the *entire* ambient type, but ours must be restricted to `J ∪ V`. Using the typeclass would either over-commit `lt` on nodes outside the carrier (a strictly stronger requirement than the LN) or force a subtype-coercion wrapper that every consumer would have to thread through. The LN literally writes "for every `v, w ∈ J ∪ V`", and the bounded form `∀ v ∈ G, …` is the cleanest faithful encoding.

**Four conjuncts in LN order; trichotomy disjuncts follow Mathlib's `Trichotomous` convention.** The conjunction is irreflexive ∧ transitive ∧ trichotomous ∧ parents-precede, matching the LN's prose so that `obtain ⟨hi, htr, htri, hp⟩ := h` reads in the same order as the source. Inside the trichotomy, the disjuncts are `lt v w ∨ v = w ∨ lt w v` (equality in the middle) to align with Mathlib's `Trichotomous` definition and ease any later lift to a subtype `Trichotomous` instance.

**Parent implication has no `v ∈ G`/`w ∈ G` guard and no `v ≠ w` precondition.** The universal `∀ v w, v ∈ G.Pa w → lt v w` is well-typed without bounded prefixes because `def_3_5`'s `Pa` body already forces `v ∈ G`, and `def_3_1`'s edge-endpoint subset clause forces `w ∈ G`. Omitting a `v ≠ w` precondition is load-bearing: when `(v, v) ∈ G.E` (a directed self-loop), the implication forces `lt v v`, which contradicts irreflexivity. So the very existence of a topological order rules out directed self-loops — exactly what `claim_3_2`'s forward direction needs.

**Only the primary form is formalised; the indexed form `J ∪ V = {v_1, …, v_K}` is not a separate predicate.** The canonical tex spells out both the strict-total-order form and the enumeration form and proves them equivalent under finiteness, but we encode only the primary form. A parallel `IsTopologicalOrderIndexed` predicate would invite spurious case splits over which form is in scope, and constructing the indexing bijection requires picking an arbitrary enumeration the LN never picks. Any consumer that wants the enumeration form can derive it on demand from the finiteness of `G.J ∪ G.V`.
